title: AI Tools Field Guide

modules.title: The Toolkit Nobody Gave You
modules.sub: Mapping the AI tool landscape for the first time
modules.description: Learners step into the role of a young journalist facing a deadline with no instructions — just a pile of unfamiliar AI tools. They survey the major categories of AI tools available today and begin building a mental map of what each type of tool is actually designed to do.

modules.title: Image Makers and Word Builders
modules.sub: Getting hands-on with creative AI tools
modules.description: Learners experiment directly with writing assistants and image generation tools, producing sample outputs for their magazine story. They compare results across tools and start noticing differences in quality, style, and what each tool seems to be good — or not so good — at.

modules.title: Picking the Right Tool for the Job
modules.sub: Matching AI tools to specific tasks and goals
modules.description: Learners work through a series of realistic journalism scenarios and decide which AI tool fits each situation best. They develop a decision-making framework, weighing factors like task type, audience, accuracy needs, and time pressure.

modules.title: When the Tool Misleads You
modules.sub: Spotting errors, bias, and unreliable AI output
modules.description: Learners examine real examples of AI tools producing confident but wrong, biased, or misleading outputs. They practise verification strategies and decide how they would fact-check or flag problems before publishing anything in the school magazine.

modules.title: Stack Your Tools: A Real Project
modules.sub: Combining multiple AI tools to complete a full task
modules.description: Learners plan and produce a complete magazine piece by deliberately combining tools — for research, drafting, image creation, and organisation. They document every tool choice they make and reflect on how the tools worked together or created friction.

modules.title: Rate It: Your AI Tool Reviews
modules.sub: Writing structured, honest evaluations of AI tools
modules.description: Learners produce their own written reviews of at least two AI tools they used during the course, following a structured evaluation format. Each review must identify the tool's strengths, a clear limitation, and a recommendation for when a classmate should or should not use it.

synopsis: A young journalist is handed a school magazine story with no guidance — just a collection of AI tools and a deadline, and every choice about which tool to use and how to verify its output is yours to make. Students explore the landscape of AI tools across writing, image generation, research, and organisation, developing real judgment about fit, quality, and risk.
rationale: Students encounter dozens of AI tools with no map for understanding what each is for, how they differ, or how to evaluate whether one is actually trustworthy and appropriate for their needs.
learning_outcome: Students can identify at least five categories of AI tools, select an appropriate tool for a given creative or academic task, and produce a structured evaluation of that tool's outputs including at least one identified limitation.
source_signals: Top 5 Copilot AI features for students
How AI study tools support students
